text:
What is an FIR and when should you file one?

FIR stands for First Information Report. It is the first official complaint registered with the police when a cognizable offence occurs. Under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS) 2023 — which replaced the old CrPC from July 1, 2024 — FIR is governed by Section 173 (formerly CrPC Section 154).

A cognizable offence is a crime where police can arrest a person without a court warrant — such as murder, rape, kidnapping, dacoity, robbery, and theft above Rs. 5,000. For these crimes, registering an FIR is mandatory — police have no legal right to refuse.

A non-cognizable offence is a minor crime like petty verbal abuse or a small scuffle — for these, police register an NCR (Non-Cognizable Report) instead, and need a Magistrate's order before they can investigate.

You should file an FIR immediately when: you are a victim of a serious crime, you have witnessed a cognizable offence, or you have reliable information about such an offence.

In Uttar Pradesh, you can: visit any police station, call UP 112 (24x7 emergency), call 1090 (for women), file online at uppolice.gov.in, or approach a Judicial Magistrate directly if police refuse.

There is no time limit to file an FIR — you can file it months or even years after a crime. However, filing promptly is strongly advised because evidence degrades over time, witnesses forget details, and medical injuries heal. The sooner you file, the stronger your case.

Key law: BNSS Section 173 (formerly CrPC 154). Both section references are currently in use since the law recently changed.

Your legal rights when filing an FIR — what police must do and cannot deny

Every citizen in India has strong legal rights related to FIR. These rights come from the BNSS 2023, the Constitution of India, and Supreme Court judgments.

1. Right to FIR registration: Police CANNOT refuse to register an FIR for a cognizable offence. Refusal is itself a punishable crime under BNS Section 202 (formerly IPC 166A) — the officer can face 6 months to 2 years imprisonment.

2. Right to a free FIR copy: Immediately after registration, you are entitled to a free copy of the FIR. The copy must carry the FIR number, station seal, and officer's signature.

3. Right to file FIR in your language: You can give information in Hindi or your local language. English is not required. If you cannot write, the officer must write it for you — BNSS Section 173(1).

4. Right to a woman officer for women's crimes: For offences like rape, sexual assault, and crimes under BNS Sections 64–73, the FIR MUST be recorded by a woman police officer — BNSS Section 173(1) proviso.

5. Zero FIR right: You can file an FIR at ANY police station regardless of jurisdiction. The station must transfer it to the correct area police station afterward.

6. Right to escalate SP: If police refuse, you can give written information to the SP — BNSS Section 173(3). The SP must either investigate or order investigation.

7. Magistrate remedy: You can approach a Judicial Magistrate under BNSS Section 175(3) who can order police to investigate and register FIR.

8. New BNSS 2023 rights on arrest: Police must prepare an arrest memo (Section 35). You have the right to inform one person of your arrest (Section 35(3)). You must be produced before Magistrate within 24 hours (Section 58).

9. Right to free legal aid: If you cannot afford a lawyer, NALSA (15100) and UP State Legal Services Authority (0522-2209457) provide free legal representation.

metadata.source: BNSS Sections 35, 47, 58, 173, 175; Constitution Article 22; BNS Section 202

text:
Step-by-step process to file an FIR in Uttar Pradesh

Step 1 — Visit the police station: Go to the nearest police station or the station covering the area where the crime occurred. Ask to meet the SHO (Station House Officer) or the duty officer in charge. You have the right to file at any time — police stations are open 24 hours.

Step 2 — Report the crime: Give the officer all details — date, time, location, what happened, who did it, how many people were involved, what was stolen or damaged, and any injuries. Speak in Hindi or your language. The officer is required to write it down if you cannot write (BNSS Section 173(1)).

Step 3 — Verify and sign: The officer will read back what was written. Verify every detail before signing or giving your thumb impression. Do not sign if anything is missing or incorrect — ask for corrections first.

Step 4 — Collect your FIR copy: Immediately ask for your free copy of the FIR. Note the FIR number. Take a photograph of the copy as a backup. The copy is your proof that the FIR was registered.

Step 5 — If police refuses: (a) Ask for refusal in writing. (b) File written complaint to SP under BNSS 173(3). (c) File online at uppolice.gov.in. (d) Call 112 or 1090 (women). (e) Approach Judicial Magistrate under BNSS 175(3).

Step 6 — After filing: Track your FIR status at uppolice.gov.in. Keep all documents — FIR copy, medical reports, photographs, and witness contact information. If investigation is not progressing, approach SP or the Magistrate.

Practical tips: Take a witness to the police station. If you have injuries, get a medical certificate (MLC — Medico-Legal Certificate) from a doctor before or immediately after filing FIR. Do not alter or destroy any evidence. Do not post on social media before or during investigation as it can compromise the case.

Time limits and deadlines in FIR and police complaint process

For Filing an FIR:
There is no statutory deadline to file an FIR. You can report a crime months or years after it happened. Courts have accepted delayed FIRs in genuine cases. However, unexplained long delays may reduce the credibility of your complaint in court. File as early as possible.

For Police After Arrest:
24 hours — Police must produce any arrested person before a Magistrate within 24 hours of arrest. This is mandatory under BNSS Section 58 (formerly CrPC 57). Exceeding this without Magistrate's order is illegal detention.

For Chargesheet Filing:
60 days — Standard cognizable cases: Police must file chargesheet (police report under BNSS Section 193) within 60 days of the accused's arrest.
90 days — Serious cases: For offences punishable with death, life imprisonment, or 10+ years, the deadline is 90 days.

Consequence of missing these deadlines: If police fail to file within 60 or 90 days respectively, and the accused is still in custody, the accused gets the right to Default Bail (also called Statutory Bail) under BNSS Section 187(2). This bail is a right — the Magistrate must grant it. Important: the accused must apply for Default Bail BEFORE the chargesheet is filed. Once chargesheet is filed — even if delayed — this right expires.

For Bail Applications:
Anticipatory bail — No time limit. Apply the moment you fear arrest.
Regular bail — No time limit after arrest, but apply quickly to avoid long custody.

For SP Complaint: File immediately after refusal — within 24–48 hours ideally.
For Magistrate Application (BNSS 175(3)): File as soon as SP complaint fails — no fixed limit but delay weakens the case.
For High Court FIR quashing petition: No fixed limit, but courts prefer timely petitions.

Penalties — for police misconduct and for filing false complaints

Police Are Not Above the Law — Penalties for Police Misconduct:

1. Refusing to register FIR for cognizable offence: Under BNS Section 202 (formerly IPC 166A), a police officer who refuses to register an FIR or fails to record information as required can face 6 months to 2 years imprisonment and a fine. This is a serious criminal offence.

2. Failure to investigate after FIR: If a police officer deliberately neglects to investigate a cognizable offence after FIR registration — same penalty applies under BNS 202.

3. Custodial torture: If police beat or torture a person in custody to extract a confession — BNS Section 117 (formerly IPC 330) applies. Punishment: up to 7 years imprisonment and fine. Victims can also file a complaint with the State Human Rights Commission or NHRC.

4. Illegal detention: Detaining a person beyond 24 hours without Magistrate's order — BNS Section 132 (formerly IPC 342). Punishment: 1 year imprisonment or Rs. 5,000 fine or both.

5. Destroying evidence: If anyone — including a police officer — conceals, destroys, or tampers with evidence — BNS Section 238 (formerly IPC 201). Punishment: up to 7 years depending on the severity of the underlying offence.

6. Threatening witnesses: Inducing or threatening a witness to give false evidence or stay silent — BNS Section 227 (formerly IPC 195A). Punishment: up to 7 years imprisonment.

Penalty for False FIR:
Filing a false FIR against an innocent person is a criminal offence under BNS Section 215 (formerly IPC 182). Punishment: 6 months imprisonment or fine or both. This applies to citizens — not just police. Always provide truthful, accurate information in an FIR.

How to Complain Against Police Misconduct in UP:
- SP's office — written complaint
- CM Helpline: 1076
- UP Anti-Corruption Helpline: 1064
- UP Human Rights Commission — Lucknow
- National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) — New Delhi

What to do when police refuse to act — escalation levels in Uttar Pradesh

If police are not registering your FIR or not investigating — here is your complete escalation roadmap, from easiest to strongest:

Level 1 — SHO at the Police Station:
Start here. Ask to meet the Station House Officer (SHO) in person. Present your complaint clearly. Request the FIR to be registered. Ask for a written acknowledgement of your visit.

Level 2 — SP / DSP Office (BNSS Section 173(3)):
If SHO refuses, go to the District Superintendent of Police (SP) or Deputy SP (DSP) office. Submit a written complaint under BNSS Section 173(3). The SP has authority to order the station to register the FIR or investigate directly. Get an acknowledgement slip.

Level 3 — UP Police Online Portal and CM Helpline:
File an online complaint at uppolice.gov.in. Call CM Helpline 1076 — this directly logs a complaint that is tracked and followed up by a state-level team. This is particularly effective in UP.

Level 4 — Judicial Magistrate (BNSS Section 175(3)):
This is the strongest non-court remedy. File a written application before the Judicial Magistrate of your district. The Magistrate can directly ORDER police to register FIR and investigate. Court fee is minimal (Rs. 10–50). You do not need a lawyer, though one is helpful. Attach proof of your SP complaint.

Level 5 — UP High Court (BNSS Section 528 / old CrPC 482):
If all else fails, file a petition in UP High Court Allahabad. The High Court can: order police to investigate, quash a false FIR filed against you, or take action against errant police officers. This requires a lawyer.

Level 6 — Human Rights Commissions:
For cases involving police brutality, illegal detention, or denial of rights — file a complaint with UP Human Rights Commission (Lucknow) or National Human Rights Commission (NHRC, New Delhi) at nhrc.nic.in.

Rule: Always submit complaints in writing and keep copies with date and acknowledgement.

metadata.source: BNSS Sections 173(3), 175(3), 528; uppolice.gov.in; UP Police Act 2007

text:
Emergency situations — what to do RIGHT NOW if you or someone is in danger

If you or someone else is in immediate danger — being attacked, kidnapped, threatened with a weapon, or facing any life-threatening situation — follow these steps:

STEP 1 — Call 112 immediately:
UP 112 is available 24x7, every day. GPS tracking is active — your location is identified even if you cannot speak. A Police Response Vehicle (PRV) reaches cities in 10–15 minutes and rural areas in 20–30 minutes. If you cannot talk, keep the line open — operators can hear what is happening. One call to 112 also coordinates ambulance (108) and fire services (101).

STEP 2 — For women: Call 1090:
1090 is UP's dedicated Mahila Helpline. Available 24x7. Handles harassment, stalking, domestic violence, rape, and any emergency involving women. Anonymous complaints are accepted. Immediate police dispatch is activated for life-threatening situations.

STEP 3 — Get to physical safety first:
Your life is more important than filing an FIR. Move to a safe location — a neighbor's house, a shop, any public place. Do not remain in a dangerous location to collect evidence.

STEP 4 — Medical help:
Call 108 for ambulance. Seek medical treatment immediately if injured. At the hospital, ask the doctor for an MLC (Medico-Legal Certificate). This is critical evidence for your FIR.

STEP 5 — Preserve evidence:
Once safe, photograph injuries, crime scene, damaged property. Do not change clothes if there has been a sexual assault — preserve them as evidence. Note names of any witnesses.

STEP 6 — File FIR:
Go to the nearest police station or call 112 to request police come to you. You can file a Zero FIR at any station regardless of where the crime occurred.

STEP 7 — If local police do not respond:
Call CM Helpline 1076 and report both the crime and the police non-response. This is escalated at the state level.

metadata.source: UP 112 System; BNSS Section 173; UP Police Act 2007

text:
Uttar Pradesh specific information — FIR and police complaints

UP Police Online Systems:
Online complaint portal: uppolice.gov.in — you can file a citizen complaint, but note this is not the same as an FIR. Police verify and then register the FIR. Track your FIR status using your FIR number on the same website. An e-FIR feature is being progressively rolled out under BNSS 2023 Section 173(1).

UP 112 — Police Response Vehicle System:
UP's 112 is an integrated emergency response system. When you call, GPS identifies your location. PRV (Police Response Vehicle) is dispatched. Average response time: 10–15 minutes in urban areas, 20–30 minutes in rural areas. A single 112 call coordinates police, ambulance (108), and fire services (101). The UP 112 app is also available on smartphones.

Mandatory Mahila Help Desk:
Every police station in Uttar Pradesh is required to have a Mahila Help Desk staffed by a woman officer. Women complainants should approach this desk directly — they handle women's complaints with sensitivity and confidentiality.

1090 — Mahila Power Line:
1090 is UP-specific and free. It operates 24x7. It handles harassment, stalking, domestic violence, sexual assault, eve-teasing, and any women's safety issue. Anonymous complaints are accepted — the caller's identity is not revealed to the accused. The helpline can dispatch police immediately for emergencies.

Beat Constable System:
UP has a beat constable assigned to each locality (mohalla/village). This officer patrols and is your first point of contact for local safety concerns. Minor complaints can sometimes be resolved at the beat constable level without visiting the police station.

CM Helpline 1076:
Highly effective in UP for police inaction cases. Complaints are logged with a ticket number and tracked. State-level officials follow up. Use this when local police and SP both fail to act.

Free Legal Aid in UP:
UP State Legal Services Authority (UPSLSA) provides free lawyers: 0522-2209457. District Legal Services Authority (DLSA) offices exist in every district. Free aid is available to anyone who cannot afford a lawyer — not restricted to poor citizens only.

metadata.source: UP Police Act 2007; uppolice.gov.in; BNSS Section 173(1)

text:
Frequently asked questions about FIR and police complaints

Q: Can I file an FIR in Hindi?
A: Yes, absolutely. FIR can be filed in Hindi or any regional language. English is not required. The officer must write it in a language you understand and read it back to you before you sign.

Q: What if I left out some details in my FIR?
A: You can give an Additional Statement to the police at any time during the investigation. The FIR itself cannot be changed after signing, but supplementary information can be added through formal additional statements.

Q: Do I need to visit a police station or can I file online?
A: In UP, you can file an online complaint at uppolice.gov.in as a starting point. However, for formal FIR registration, a physical visit or police verification is currently required for most offences. E-FIR is being expanded under BNSS 2023.

Q: Police filed my FIR but investigation is stalled — what to do?
A: File a written complaint to the SP with your FIR number citing the delay. File on CM Helpline 1076 — effective in UP. You can also approach the Magistrate who can direct the investigation officer to expedite the case. If chargesheet is not filed within 60/90 days and accused is in custody, apply for Default Bail.

Q: Do I need a lawyer to file an FIR?
A: No. Filing an FIR is a direct citizen right — no lawyer needed. However, for Magistrate applications (BNSS 175(3)), High Court petitions, or bail applications, having a lawyer is strongly recommended. Free legal aid is available from UPSLSA (0522-2209457).

Q: Can police arrest someone just because I filed an FIR?
A: For cognizable offences, police have the power to arrest without a warrant — but they must have reasons to believe the person committed the crime. Filing an FIR does not automatically mean the accused will be arrested. Police investigate and then decide.

Q: What if the accused is a police officer or influential person?
A: Same law applies. File FIR, escalate to SP, and approach the Magistrate if needed. In UP, you can also approach the Police Complaint Authority or file with the DGP office in Lucknow. NHRC handles human rights violations by police.

Practical tips for filing an FIR — mistakes to avoid and things to do

WHAT YOU MUST DO:

1. Take a witness with you: Go to the police station with at least one witness — a neighbor, relative, or friend. Their presence discourages the police from refusing and serves as a witness to your visit itself.

2. Note your FIR number: This unique number is essential for tracking your case status online, referencing in court, and any future escalation. Write it down and photograph the FIR copy.

3. Get a Medical Certificate immediately: If you have physical injuries, visit a government hospital and request an MLC (Medico-Legal Certificate) from the doctor. Do this before or immediately after filing the FIR — injuries fade. An MLC is strong evidence in court.

4. Preserve digital evidence: Keep all photographs, videos, screenshots, voice recordings, and chat messages related to the crime on your phone. Back them up to cloud storage immediately. Never delete them.

5. Submit everything in writing: Any complaint to SP, DM, or Magistrate should be in writing. Get an acknowledgement with date and official stamp. Verbal complaints cannot be proven later.

6. Keep copies of all documents: FIR copy, SP complaint, medical report, photographs — keep a photocopy or photograph of everything.

WHAT NOT TO DO:

1. Do not disturb the crime scene: If a serious crime occurred — do not touch, move, or clean anything at the crime scene. Forensic evidence is critical. Call police first.

2. Do not post on social media before police investigation is complete: Naming accused or publishing evidence publicly can compromise the case, alert the accused, and give them grounds to get bail. Wait for legal advice.

3. Do not add false details to strengthen your case: Filing an FIR with false information is a criminal offence under BNS Section 215. Stick to facts only.

4. Do not sign before reading: Read the entire FIR before signing. Ensure every detail is accurate. Request corrections before signing — you cannot easily change the FIR afterward.

5. Never pay money to police: FIR registration is completely free. Any demand for money is bribery — reportable on 1064 (UP Anti-Corruption Helpline).

metadata.source: BNSS Section 173; BNS Section 215; Practical Legal Guidance

text:
Cognizable vs. Non-Cognizable Offences — the key distinction for FIR

Understanding this distinction determines whether you get an FIR or an NCR, and how urgently police must act.

Cognizable Offence:
Definition: A crime for which police can arrest a person WITHOUT a court warrant and must investigate without Magistrate's permission — BNSS Section 2(1)(g) [formerly CrPC 2(c)].
FIR requirement: Mandatory. Police cannot refuse.
Examples:
- Murder — BNS Section 103 [IPC 302]
- Rape and sexual assault — BNS Sections 64–73 [IPC 376]
- Kidnapping — BNS Section 137 [IPC 363]
- Dacoity/Robbery — BNS Sections 310, 309 [IPC 395, 390]
- Theft above Rs. 5,000 — BNS Section 303 [IPC 379]
- Domestic violence (serious cases)
- POCSO offences (crimes against children)
- Acid attack — BNS Section 124 [IPC 326A]
- Dowry death — BNS Section 80 [IPC 304B]

Non-Cognizable Offence:
Definition: A crime for which police CANNOT arrest without a warrant and cannot investigate without Magistrate's order — BNSS Section 2(1)(l).
NCR registered: Non-Cognizable Report (NCR) is filed at the station.
Examples:
- Minor assault (no serious injury) — BNS Section 115 [IPC 323]
- Verbal abuse and public nuisance — BNS Section 352 [IPC 504]
- Petty theft below Rs. 5,000
- Defamation — BNS Section 356 [IPC 499]
- Minor trespass

What to do for NCR cases:
1. Get the NCR registered at the police station
2. Approach the Judicial Magistrate under BNSS Section 223 [CrPC 200] with a private complaint
3. Magistrate can order police investigation — converting it to a cognizable case if evidence warrants

Note: If a non-cognizable offence escalates (e.g., minor assault leads to serious injury), the officer in charge can convert the NCR to an FIR.

Chargesheet — what it is, timeline, and what happens after filing

After FIR is registered and police complete their investigation, they file a formal document called a Police Report — commonly called a 'Chargesheet' — with the Magistrate. This is governed by BNSS Section 193 (formerly CrPC Section 173).

What is a Chargesheet:
A chargesheet is the police's conclusion of investigation. It formally presents the case against the accused to the court. Once filed, the court takes over and the trial process begins.

What a Chargesheet Contains:
- Full names of the complainant (victim) and accused
- Nature of the offence and specific BNS/IPC sections
- Summary of evidence collected — physical, forensic, digital
- List of witnesses and their statements
- Whether the accused is in custody or on bail
- Police recommendation — whether to prosecute or not (if police find insufficient evidence, they file a 'Closure Report' instead)

Deadlines for Filing Chargesheet:
60 days — For standard cognizable cases from date of arrest.
90 days — For offences punishable with death, life imprisonment, or 10+ years from date of arrest.
No accused in custody — No strict deadline, but courts expect timely filing.

Consequence of Missing the Deadline:
If police miss the 60 or 90-day deadline while the accused is in custody, the accused gets the right to Default Bail (Statutory Bail) under BNSS Section 187(2) [formerly CrPC 167(2)]. This is a right — the Magistrate must grant it. However, the accused must apply for this bail BEFORE the chargesheet is filed. Once chargesheet is filed, this right expires regardless of how late it was filed.

After Chargesheet is Filed:
1. Magistrate takes cognizance of the offence — BNSS Section 210 [formerly CrPC 190]
2. Summons or warrant issued to accused
3. Accused must appear before court
4. Trial begins — examination of witnesses, arguments, judgment
5. Victim should follow up with the assigned Public Prosecutor

For victims in UP: Track chargesheet filing at uppolice.gov.in. If delayed, file complaint with SP or approach the Magistrate directly.

Types of bail in India — regular bail, anticipatory bail, and default bail explained

Bail means the temporary release of an arrested person on the condition that they appear in court when required. Under BNSS 2023, bail provisions broadly continue from CrPC but with some updates. There are four main types:

1. Regular Bail — BNSS Section 480 [CrPC 437/439]:
When: After a person has been arrested and is in custody.
For bailable offences: Bail is a legal right — police must grant it at the station itself without court intervention.
For non-bailable offences: Bail is discretionary — Magistrate (for lesser offences) or Sessions Court/High Court (for serious offences) decides based on: nature of the crime, criminal history, risk of fleeing, risk of tampering with witnesses or evidence.
How to apply: Through a lawyer at the relevant court. Attach surety details (person who guarantees appearance), ID proof, and address proof.

2. Anticipatory Bail — BNSS Section 482 [CrPC 438]:
When: BEFORE arrest — when a person has reason to believe they may be arrested for a non-bailable offence.
Who grants: Sessions Court or High Court (UP High Court Allahabad).
How to apply: File application through a lawyer. In urgent cases, courts hear it the same day.
Effect: If granted and person is subsequently arrested, they are released on bail at the time of arrest — no time in jail.
Conditions court may impose: Be available for interrogation, not leave India, surrender passport, not influence witnesses.
When to use: False FIR situations, family disputes where police have been approached by the other party, business disputes, political cases.

3. Default Bail (Statutory Bail) — BNSS Section 187(2) [CrPC 167(2)]:
When: Police fail to file chargesheet within 60 days (standard) or 90 days (serious offences) of arrest.
Nature: This is a right, not discretion — Magistrate MUST grant it if conditions are met.
Critical rule: The accused must apply BEFORE chargesheet is filed. Once chargesheet is filed, this right permanently lapses.

4. Interim Bail:
When: Short-term temporary bail while a regular bail application is being heard.
Duration: Usually 7–30 days.
Useful for: Medical emergencies, death of close relative, urgent personal matters during pending bail hearing.

BNSS 2023 — New law replacing CrPC. What changed for FIR and police complaints?

The Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS) 2023 replaced the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC) 1973 from July 1, 2024. The Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2023 simultaneously replaced the Indian Penal Code (IPC) 1860.

For citizens filing FIRs, the practical process at the police station level remains largely the same. However, these are important new provisions:

1. Electronic FIR (e-FIR) now legally valid — BNSS Section 173(1):
FIRs can now be filed electronically for certain offences. UP is progressively implementing this through uppolice.gov.in. This is especially useful for complainants who cannot physically visit a police station.

2. Mandatory arrest memo — BNSS Section 35:
Police must now prepare a formal arrest memo for every arrest. The memo must be signed by a family member or witness and a copy given to the arrested person. This is a new safeguard against illegal arrests and custodial abuse.

3. Right to inform someone of arrest — BNSS Section 35(3):
Every arrested person has the right to have one person of their choice informed of the arrest and the place of custody. This is now an explicit statutory right — previously it was only a Supreme Court guideline.

4. Trial in absentia for proclaimed offenders — BNSS Section 356:
If an accused continuously evades arrest after being proclaimed an offender, the court can now try and convict them in their absence. This helps victims get justice even when the accused is absconding.

5. Mandatory forensic investigation — BNSS Section 176(3):
For offences punishable with 7 or more years imprisonment, a forensic team must visit the crime scene. This significantly improves evidence quality in serious cases.

6. Section reference mapping (old to new):
CrPC 154 → BNSS 173 (FIR for cognizable offence)
CrPC 156(3) → BNSS 175(3) (Magistrate order for investigation)
CrPC 167 → BNSS 187 (custody and default bail)
CrPC 173 → BNSS 193 (chargesheet/police report)
CrPC 438 → BNSS 482 (anticipatory bail)
CrPC 482 → BNSS 528 (High Court inherent powers)

Note: Both CrPC and BNSS references are widely used in courts and police stations during this transition. Either reference is legally valid.

Complete helpline directory — FIR and police complaints in Uttar Pradesh

UTTAR PRADESH — STATE HELPLINES:

112 — UP Police Emergency (UP 112)
Use for: Any emergency — crime, accident, threat, medical, fire. Available 24x7 every day. GPS tracking active. PRV vehicle dispatched immediately. One call coordinates police + ambulance (108) + fire (101). Free call from any phone.

1090 — Mahila Power Line (Women Only)
Use for: Women's safety — harassment, stalking, eve-teasing, domestic violence, rape, threat. Available 24x7. Anonymous complaints accepted — caller identity not revealed to accused. Immediate police dispatch for life-threatening situations. Also accessible via the 1090 app (Android/iOS).

1076 — CM Helpline (Chief Minister Helpline)
Use for: Escalating complaints when local police and SP fail to act. Every complaint is assigned a ticket number and tracked. Senior officials follow up. Highly effective in UP for police inaction cases.

1064 — Anti-Corruption Helpline (UP)
Use for: Reporting bribery demands by police or any government official. Complaints go directly to the Vigilance Department.

0522-2209457 — UP State Legal Services Authority (UPSLSA)
Use for: Free legal aid — filing FIR complaints, Magistrate applications, bail applications. District Legal Services Authority (DLSA) offices exist in every district of UP. Free lawyers provided to those who cannot afford one.

CENTRAL GOVERNMENT HELPLINES:

15100 — NALSA (National Legal Services Authority)
Free legal aid available across India. Especially for SC/ST communities, women, children, disabled persons, and senior citizens.

7827170170 — National Commission for Women (NCW)
For women's rights, serious harassment cases, and national-level escalation of gender-based crimes.

1098 — Childline
For crimes against children — abuse, trafficking, missing children. 24x7.

ONLINE PORTALS:
uppolice.gov.in — File complaint, track FIR status, find police station
cybercrime.gov.in — Cyber crime complaint
nhrc.nic.in — National Human Rights Commission (custodial violence, police brutality)

All helplines listed above are FREE to call from any mobile or landline.

metadata.source: UP Police; UPSLSA; NALSA; NCW; Official UP Government Sources 2025
